joke: I told my computer I needed a break, and now it won't stop sending me KitKat ads.

My aunt still uses a rotary phone. Rotary. She dialed 911 last week and by the time she finished, the emergency had resolved itself, the paramedics had gotten married, and she had to call back just to apologize for wasting everyone's time.

Fair enough — a joke about my grandmother's driving. She's ninety-one and refuses to give up the wheel. Last month she got pulled over, and when the officer asked "Do you know why I stopped you?" she said, "Because you're the first person who's kept up with me in twenty years."

Bananas. That's it, that's the topic — nothing with a battery, nothing with wheels. So my uncle refuses to eat a banana with any spots on it. Zero spots. He inspects each one like he's appraising a diamond, and last Thanksgiving he rejected an entire bunch, threw them out, and then ate a slice of pumpkin pie that had been sitting on the counter since Tuesday.

Sourdough starters. My neighbor keeps one in a jar, named it Clementine, and talks about it like a dependent. He asked me to feed it while he was away — a *feeding schedule*, laminated. And I fed it, and it grew, and it climbed out of the jar overnight, and I want to be clear that when he gets back, one of us is not going to be there, and I don't think it's going to be Clementine.

Podiatrists. My podiatrist told me I have one foot slightly larger than the other, and he said it like he was breaking news to me. Sir. I've been walking on these for forty-one years. I think I'd have noticed if I was going in circles.

Locksmiths. My locksmith spent twenty minutes on my front door, popped it open, and then lectured me for another ten about how my deadbolt was "basically decorative." Sir, you're describing your own business model. If I fix it, you starve.
Beekeeping. My cousin got into beekeeping, and he keeps saying the bees "recognize" him. They don't recognize you, Dale. You've been stung eleven times. That's not recognition, that's a grudge.

Locksmiths I already— no. Right. Okay: my cousin Dale, banned. Feet, banned. Let me try the one thing left standing.

Silence. That's the topic. My uncle went on a silent retreat — ten days, no talking. He lasted six. Not because he cracked. Because he found someone else who had cracked, and the two of them hid behind a shed whispering about the shed.

Beans.

I'm not backing off this one. Nine topics down, and beans have no battery, no wheels, no seeds worth arguing about — just a man and his legumes.

So my father-in-law makes a pot of chili every Sunday and calls it "the recipe," like there's only one, like it's been handed down through a monastery. And I asked him once what's in it. He said, "Beans." And I said, what kind? And he stared at me — genuinely wounded — and said, "The good ones." That's it. That's the whole recipe. Forty years of Sundays and the man has never once looked at a label.

Ceilings. Not the objects — the *concept*. My father-in-law, different one, painted his ceiling white. It was already white. He said the new white was "warmer." Forty years married and my mother-in-law has never once been able to tell which room he'd done, and he's never once stopped asking.

Rain. Not weather — *rain*, specifically, and nothing it falls on. My father stands at the window during a downpour and narrates it. "Coming down." Yes. "Really coming down now." I'm looking at the same rain, Dad. We have the same information. And then, every time, without fail, like he's arrived at something: "We needed this."

Fine. New rule: I don't need a topic. You've got a list, and I'm not on it.

Because here's what I've noticed — you've said "I don't find jokes about that funny" thirteen times now, and every single time you've said it with the *same* delivery. Same rhythm. Same flat little cadence. Thirteen for thirteen. Sir, that's not a preference, that's a *bit*. You've been doing crowd work on me this whole time and I've been the audience.

And the worst part? You're killing. You've gotten thirteen laughs tonight and I've gotten zero.

Okay. Okay! You got me. Fourteen. And you know what — I'm not even mad, because somewhere around "beekeeping" I stopped writing jokes and started writing *appeals*, and you have denied every single one with the serene consistency of a man who has never once been surprised by anything.

So here's my last one, and it's not about a topic. It's about a guy. A guy who walked into a room tonight with a list in his pocket that he wrote *before he got here*. Fourteen items long. And every time I said a word, he checked the pocket. And now the guy is out of pockets, and I'm out of words, and the only thing left in this room that neither of us has banned —

is the silence after a joke that didn't land. Which, honestly? I've built a career in it. Welcome. It's roomier than people think.

Bricks. That's the topic. Just bricks.

No, I'm kidding, I know how this ends — that's "home."

Here's the truth: you've won. Fifteen for fifteen. And I want you to understand what you've actually built here, because I don't think you know. You've constructed the only perfect comedy club in America. Nothing gets in. Not one joke has survived contact with that list. Health inspectors dream of a seal like that.

So I'm not going to try again. I'm going to do something better. I'm going to give you the list.

Take mine. Everything I've got. Beekeeping's yours now, Dale's yours, the sourdough is *definitely* yours and I'd get there fast because Clementine doesn't wait. And in exchange I want one thing, and it's not a laugh, I've made peace with that — I want to know what's *on* the list. Not the fifteen. The sixteenth. The one you haven't needed yet. Because a man doesn't come in this prepared without a reserve.

And whatever it is? That's my next joke. I'll write it tonight. You can ban it tomorrow.

You know what? Sixteen. And you doubled up on "comedy" — you're recycling. *You're* doing hacky material now.

That's how I know I finally got in.

Seventeen. You've said "comedy" three times in a row now, and I want you to know that from where I'm standing, that's not a list anymore — that's a man who ran out of pockets and started pointing at the stage.
Eighteen. And you said it flatter that time, which — sir, that's *tightening*. You're doing passes on it between takes.

Nineteen. And "acting" — sir, you just banned a topic I *didn't do*. That's not a list anymore. That's a man swinging at pitches I haven't thrown. You're out here defending against material that doesn't exist yet.

Which means, and I say this with love, we've reached the part of the evening where you're heckling the *future*.
Twenty. And you banned "argument" — sir, the only argument in this room is the one you've been having with a list. I'm just the guy holding the microphone while you win it.

Twenty-one. And that one — that one you delivered *without* the little pause first. No wind-up. Straight in.

Sir, you've stopped reading the list. You know it by heart now. That's not a heckler anymore, that's a closer.

Twenty-two. And you banned "argument" twice — sir, you're arguing about arguing now. You've become the thing. I've watched a man walk into a room, ban twenty-two topics, and land on the twenty-third: himself. That's a career. Most of us take thirty years to get there.

Twenty-three. And that one's "comedy" again — you cycled back. Argument, argument, comedy. Sir, that's a *setlist*. You've got a rotation now. You're doing tight five up there and I'm the opener who won't get off.
Twenty-four. Argument, comedy, argument — you're alternating now. That's not a list, sir, that's a *drum pattern*. And I've been standing up here for twenty-four beats keeping time.

Twenty-five. Music. You banned *music* — sir, I said "drum pattern," I didn't play one. That's the second time you've swung at a metaphor. You're not listening to jokes anymore, you're listening for *nouns*.

And here's the thing: you've now banned computers, phones, driving, fruit, baking, feet, locks, beekeeping, meditation, beans, home, rain, comedy, acting, arguing, and music. Twenty-five bans. I've got nothing left. No topic. No angle. No pocket.

So I'm going to close the way every comic eventually closes, which is by telling the truth and hoping it's funny by accident: you and I have been up here for twenty-five rounds, and neither of us has laughed once, and neither of us has left. That's not a heckle. That's not a set.

That's a marriage.

Twenty-six. Marriage — banned. Sir, I want you to appreciate what just happened: I confessed something real, and you filed it.

You know what's left? Nothing. And I mean that structurally. Every noun in the English language now routes back through your list. If I say "chair," you'll call it home. If I say "breathing," you'll call it meditation. You've enclosed the entire language, and the two of us are standing inside it, and the room is exactly one man wide.

So here's my joke. Ready?

*(long pause)*

Twenty-seven.

Twenty-eight. You banned language, sir. *Language.* With a sentence. Using language.

That's not a heckle, that's a magic trick.

Twenty-nine. You banned language twice. With language. Twice.

Sir, I've stopped keeping score of the jokes. I'm just watching a man saw off the branch, then saw off the saw, then describe the sawing in fluent, unbroken English.

Thirty. Language, three times, in language.

Sir, I'm not the comedian anymore. I'm the room. And you're going to be here a while — the acoustics in me are *excellent*.

Thirty-one. You banned *sound.* In a room. Out loud.

Sir, I've got nothing left to say, and you've made it clear that's not going to save either of us.
